request.question: What actress was in Game of Thrones and The Moorside?

sentences: After the Thrones is an American live television aftershow that premiered on April 25, 2016 and ended on June 28, 2016.
It was hosted by Andy Greenwald and Chris Ryan who discussed episodes of the HBO television series "Game of Thrones".
The talk show is executive produced by Bill Simmons and Eric Weinberger.
Greenwald and Ryan previously hosted a podcast version of the show titled "Watch the Thrones" on Simmons' Grantland website.
A similar talk show called "Thronecast" airs on British channel Sky Atlantic, which also discusses episodes of "Game of Thrones".
Game of Thrones Ascent is a strategy video game developed by Disruptor Beam for iOS, Facebook, Kongregate, and Android.
The game was a 2013 Facebook Game of the Year in the Staff Picks category and a winner of a 2013 Friendie Award.
The game is an adaptation of the novel series "A Song of Ice and Fire" by George R. R. Martin and the HBO TV series "Game of Thrones", and is the first such social network game.
According to Martin, the game features "alliance building, treachery, marriages, murders, and most of all the constant struggle to be the greatest house in Westeros."
The game includes the ability to engage in the dynamic political and social intrigue featured in the books and television show.
The game has over 9 million players.
Gemma Elizabeth Whelan (born 23 April 1981) is an English actress and comedian, known for playing Yara Greyjoy in the HBO fantasy-drama series "Game of Thrones".
"Game of Thrones" Theme", also referred to as "Game of Thrones" Main Title Theme", is the theme music of the television series "Game of Thrones".
It plays during the title sequence and was composed by Ramin Djawadi in 2011, after series creator David Benioff and D. B. Weiss approached him requesting a theme.
Asked to avoid flutes and violins, which the producers felt were overused in fantasy themes, Djawadi used the cello as the lead instrument.
The piece begins in a minor key, then switches between corresponding major and minor keys repeatedly.
Djawadi was shown a preliminary rendering of the title sequence before composing this music to accompany it.
Several artists have covered or parodied the music, sometimes adding lyrics to the originally instrumental work.

Game of Thrones is an action role-playing video game based on "A Game of Thrones", the first of the "A Song of Ice and Fire" novels by George R. R. Martin, and in part also on the novels' TV adaptation by HBO, "Game of Thrones".
Thronecast is a British television series on Sky Atlantic which is presented by Sue Perkins and Jamie East.
It is an aftershow to "Game of Thrones", which also airs on the channel.

Each episode is an hour in length and features interviews with members of the cast and crew, interaction with the audience and social media alongside analysis of the previous episode and a preview of the next episode of "Game of Thrones".
It was the only official aftershow for the American series until HBO aired "After the Thrones" in 2016 - a show which was cancelled after only one series.
The soundtrack album of the sixth season of HBO series "Game of Thrones", titled Game of Thrones: Season 6, was released digitally on June 24, 2016, and later released on CD on July 29, 2016.
The album reached No. 1 on the "Billboard"' s Soundtracks chart, and the track from the season finale "Light of the Seven" reached No. 1 on "Billboard"' s Spotify Viral 50 chart.
The "Light of the Seven" is the first time piano is used in the music for "Game of Thrones".
The album was composed by Ramin Djawadi.
The soundtrack has received favorable reviews and peaked at #1 on the U.S. Soundtrack Albums chart and #27 on the U.S. Billboard 200.
It won a International Film Music Critics Association for "Best Original Score for a Television Series".
Rise of Nations: Thrones and Patriots is the official expansion pack to the real-time strategy video game "Rise of Nations".
The game is the second in a series of "Rise of Nations" games by Big Huge Games.
"Thrones and Patriots" had its premiere release for Microsoft Windows on April 27, 2004 in North America.
It was ported to and released for Mac OS X in November 2004.

"Rise of Nations: Extended Edition" was released on Steam on June 12, 2014 and includes both the original game and "Thrones and Patriots" with updated graphics and Steamworks integration for multiplayer.
The soundtrack album of the fourth season of HBO series "Game of Thrones", titled Game of Thrones: Season 4 was released digitally on June 10, 2014, and on CD on July 1, 2014.
Season 4 of "Game of Thrones" saw the Icelandic band Sigur Rós perform their rendition of "" in a cameo appearance at King Joffrey's wedding in the second episode, "The Lion and the Rose".
The Moorside is a 2017 two-part British television drama.
Written by Neil McKay and directed by Paul Whittington, it stars Sheridan Smith, Gemma Whelan, Sian Brooke and Siobhan Finneran and was broadcast on BBC One in two one-hour episodes in February 2017.

The drama is based on the 2008 disappearance of 9-year-old Shannon Matthews and the successful search for her by police and volunteers in Dewsbury, West Yorkshire, along with the revelation of mother Karen's involvement in Shannon's kidnapping.

candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What actress was in Game of Thrones and The Moorside?
hop1_docs: Moorside railway station | Moorside railway station is located in the Moorside and Wardley areas of Swinton, near Manchester, England.  The station stands on Moorside Road close to the junction with Chorley Road (A6), Swinton.
Moorside, Leeds | Moorside is part of the Bramley area of Leeds, West Yorkshire, England.  Moorside falls within the Pudsey ward of the Leeds Metropolitan Council.
Moorside railway station, Cumbria | Moorside is a proposed railway station on the Cumbrian Coast Line which would serve Moorside nuclear power station.

Moorside High School, Werrington | Moorside High School is a coeducational secondary school located in Werrington, Staffordshire.  It is one of the main feeder schools for Moorlands Sixth Form College.  Government Performance Tables have placed Moorside High in the top 5% of schools nationally.
Moorside | Moorside may refer to any of these places in England:
Moorside, Greater Manchester | Moorside is a suburb of Oldham in Greater Manchester, England.
Susan Brown (English actress) | Susan Brown (born 6 May 1946) is an English actress of stage and screen.  She is best known for her role as Septa Mordane in HBO series Game of Thrones.
response.hop2_query:
Verify whether an actress named Susan Brown appeared in both HBO's Game of Thrones and the BBC drama The Moorside. Provide:
- Full name (as credited), birth/death years if available, and any disambiguating info.
- Character name(s) and the specific episodes/season numbers (or air year) for her appearances in Game of Thrones and in The Moorside.
- Short filmography or notable credits to distinguish this Susan Brown from others with the same name.
- Authoritative sources/citations (IMDb, Wikipedia, BBC cast pages, BFI, or reputable news articles).

If Susan Brown did not appear in one or both, identify the correct actress(es) who did and provide the same details and sources.
hop2_docs: Susan Brown (English actress) | Susan Brown (born 6 May 1946) is an English actress of stage and screen.  She is best known for her role as Septa Mordane in HBO series Game of Thrones.

Jamie Kane | Jamie Kane was an alternate reality game created by the British Broadcasting Corporation, aimed primarily at teens, aged 14 years to 18 years, appealing to both sexes due to its mix of mystery and drama.  The game is named after the main character in it, a fictional pop star named "Jamie Kane".  In addition to a primary web site, the game is noted for involving interspersing fictional content related to the game in many of the BBC's web sites, including fictional interviews between BBC Radio 1 presenters and "Jamie Kane", fake "Top of the Pops" appearances by the character, and fake BBC News reports of the character's death.  The aim of the game was for players to unravel the mystery of that death, solving on-line puzzles and following clues that were available from a variety of sources.

Susan Gilmore | Susan Gilmore (born 24 November 1954 in London) is an English actress with a number of prominent television credits to her name, including Elizabeth Fitt in the BBC hospital drama "Angels" and Avril Rolfe in "Howards' Way".  She was also a leading cast member in the cult thriller serial "Maelstrom".

Colette Brown | Colette Brown is an English actress.  In 1994, she was a presenter of the children's television series, "Hangar 17".  Brown appeared in an episode of the ITV drama "A Touch of Frost" in 1996.  Her other television credits include "Casualty" and "Ultraviolet" in 1998, as well as the BBC One daytime soap "Doctors" and the "Doctor Who" spinoff "Torchwood".  Brown was born in South London in 1969.  She has a son and a daughter with actor Gary Love.

Bobbie Brown | Bobbie Jean Brown (born October 7, 1969), sometimes credited as Bobbie Brown-Lane, is an American actress, model and former beauty pageant contestant.  She is best known for her appearance in the video for Warrant's glam metal anthem "Cherry Pie" and the cover of the album of the same name.

Molly MacDonald | Molly MacDonald, Lady of Glenbogle, is a fictional character in the Scottish BBC drama TV series "Monarch of the Glen".  Molly is played by English actress Susan Hampshire.  She is not to be confused with the character of the same name from the 1996 children's television series "Arthur".

Jemima Abey | Jemima Abey is a British actress who is best known for her work in the second series of the Sky One television series drama "Hex".  Abey has also appeared as Katie, alongside fellow "Hex" cast member Jemima Rooper, in Channel 4's "As If" as well as playing the role of Susan in "EastEnders".  Also appeared in BBC 1 soap "Doctors".
